title: Behavioral: Success & Failure (STAR Method)
body:
🏆 Success Story:

Situation: The business problem or context.

Task: Your specific role and objective.

Action: What YOU did (focus on "I" instead of "We", how you aligned the team, how you utilized data).

Result: Numbers. (Revenue generated, time saved, specific metric improvement).


📉 Failure / Conflict Story:

Situation/Task: A feature launch that failed or a conflict with R&D.

Action: How you identified the failure (Analytics!) and how you handled it with management and the team.

Result: The consequences and impact.


Learning: The most crucial part - what you learned and how you have adjusted your working process since then.

title: Product Sense:
body:
1. Clarify & Context: Ask questions! What is the goal? (Revenue, Engagement, Retention?). Who is the main competitor? Are there technical constraints?


2. Target Audience: Who are the users? (e.g., Solo travelers, families, budget backpackers). Select ONE primary persona.


3. User Pain Points/Needs: List 2-3 specific problems this persona faces. Prioritize the biggest problem.


4. Brainstorm Solutions: Propose 3 solutions (1 safe, 1 innovative, 1 "moonshot"). Choose the best one based on Impact vs. Effort.


5. Metrics & Trade-offs: How will we measure success? (Primary metric, Secondary metric, Counter-metric). What are the risks?

title: Product Analytics:
body:
1. Clarify the Metric (Isolate the problem)

  • Timeframe: Is this a sudden cliff drop or a gradual decline over days/weeks?
  • Platform: Is it happening across all platforms, or isolated to Web, Mobile Web, iOS, or Android?
  • Version: If it's the app, is it happening only on a specific app version or a specific OS (e.g., iOS 17)?
  • Geography: Is this a global drop, or isolated to a specific region/country? (e.g., "Only users in Thailand?").
  • User Segment: Is it affecting all users, or only specific segments? (e.g., Logged-in vs. Guests, VIP users, New vs. Returning).
  • Data Validity: Is the data accurate? Could this just be a broken dashboard or an analytics reporting glitch?


2. Internal Factors (Did WE break it?)

  • Releases: Did we deploy any new code, feature, or UI change right before the drop started?
  • Tracking: Is the analytics pipeline working? (e.g., Did a developer accidentally delete the tracking event?).
  • Cannibalization: Did we launch a new feature or promotion that is stealing traffic from this specific funnel?
  • Marketing: Did the marketing team pause or change a massive paid acquisition campaign today?
  • System Health: Are we seeing an increase in server errors, API timeouts, or crash rates in our backend logs?


3. External Factors (Did the OUTSIDE WORLD break it?)

  • Third-Party Services: Are there outages in services we rely on? (e.g., AWS is down, Stripe/Payment gateway is failing, Google Maps API is broken).
  • Seasonality: Is this normal behavior? (e.g., Let's compare this to the same day last week, or the same holiday last year).
  • Competitors: Did a major competitor (like Booking.com or Expedia) just launch a massive aggressive campaign or discount?
  • Macro Events: Are there global or local events affecting travel right now? (e.g., Extreme weather, political unrest, massive flight cancellations).
  • Tech Ecosystem: Did Google change their search algorithm? Did Apple release an OS update that blocks our cookies?


4. Action Plan (How to solve it)

  • Hypothesize: Based on the answers, state your strongest guess. ("Since it's only on iOS and started this morning, I suspect our latest app release broke the payment button.")
  • Validate: State how you will prove it. ("I will look at the exact error logs for the iOS payment event.")
  • Mitigate (Short-term): Fix the bleeding immediately. ("I will ask R&D to roll back the iOS release to the previous stable version.")
  • Monitor (Long-term): Prevent it from happening again. ("I will set up automated Slack alerts for whenever this specific conversion metric drops by more than 5%.")

title: Product execution
body:
1. Clarify the Goal (The "Why")

  • Business Goal: What are we trying to achieve? Is this for User Acquisition, Retention, Engagement, or Monetization? (e.g., "Are we trying to get more bookings, or increase the average order value?").
  • Constraints: Are there any time, technical, or budget constraints I should be aware of?
  • Scope: Are we focusing on Mobile App, Web, or both? A specific region?


2. Target Audience (The "Who")

  • Brainstorm Segments: Who are the potential users? (e.g., Solo backpackers, business travelers, families with young kids, luxury travelers).
  • Prioritize ONE Segment: "For this exercise, I want to focus on Families with young kids, because they have the most friction during booking and a high lifetime value."


3. User Pain Points (The "What")

  • List the Problems: What is hard for this specific user?
  • Pain point 1: It's hard to know if a hotel is truly child-friendly.
  • Pain point 2: Booking connecting rooms is usually a nightmare.
  • Pain point 3: They need flexible cancellation policies due to kids getting sick.
  • Prioritize the Problem: "I will prioritize the problem of booking connecting rooms, because it's a massive pain point with very few good solutions in the market today.


4. Brainstorm Solutions (The "How")

Give 3 distinct ideas to show flexibility, then choose one.

  • Idea 1 (Safe & Fast): A simple filter checkbox for "Guaranteed Connecting Rooms".
  • Idea 2 (Innovative): A visual floor plan where users can literally select two rooms next to each other, like buying airplane seats.
  • Idea 3 (Moonshot/AI): An AI agent that builds a full family itinerary and automatically contacts hotels to guarantee connecting rooms.
  • Select the Winner: "I'll go with the Visual Floor Plan (Idea 2). It has high impact on user experience and solves the trust issue completely, even if the effort is a bit higher."


5. MVP & Rollout Strategy (The "Agoda Way")

  • The Lean MVP: What is the fastest way to test our riskiest assumption without building the whole floor plan? ("Maybe we start by manually offering it via email to a small group of users who search for 4+ people").
  • Rollout: "I would run a strict A/B test on 5% of traffic in one specific region (like Thailand) to see how users interact with it before a global rollout."


6. Metrics & Trade-offs (Measuring Success)

  • North Star Metric (Primary): The conversion rate of users who search for family rooms and complete a booking.
  • Secondary Metric: Engagement with the new visual floor plan UI (Clicks, time spent).
  • Counter-Metric (The Trade-off): Did we hurt something else? (e.g., "We increased family bookings, but did we increase customer service calls because hotels didn't actually honor the connecting rooms?").
